one_line_takeaway: Because DPO trains the deployed policy directly on human preference pairs, it memorizes and leaks that preference data far more than PPO; the paper's PREMIA reference-based attack (including a new full-tuple variant) drives DPO membership AUROC above 0.9 while PPO stays near chance.
key_finding: Across every model and both datasets, DPO-aligned models are substantially more vulnerable to MIA than PPO-aligned models, and PPO models are nearly impregnable (AUROC ~0.50-0.55 across all attacks). PREMIA, especially the SFT-reference variant, is the strongest attack, and the novel tuple-level attack is the most revealing: e.g., on Stack-Exchange with Mistral-7B the DPO MIAPair AUROC is 0.93 vs 0.52 for PPO, and GPT2-xl reaches 0.928 tuple AUROC for DPO. The size/vulnerability relationship is task-dependent (unlike pretraining MIA where vulnerability rises with size): on the harder SE task larger GPT2 variants are MORE vulnerable, but on the easy IMDB sentiment task large models generalize well and MIA is weak (Mistral-7B DPO IMDB tuple AUROC only 0.556). DPO gives no utility advantage over PPO (similar reward) yet leaks far more, exposing a privacy-utility asymmetry favoring PPO.
core_contribution: The paper studies membership inference attacks (MIA) against the PREFERENCE DATA used to align LLMs, a data format prior MIA work had not targeted. It makes two of its own contributions: (1) a theoretical argument that DPO-aligned models are intrinsically more vulnerable to MIA than PPO-aligned models, because DPO optimizes the final policy directly on the preference data and therefore overfits it more, whereas in PPO the deployed policy is trained against a learned reward model rather than the data directly; and (2) PREMIA (PREference-data MIA), a reference-based attack framework tailored to preference data that scores membership both for individual prompt-response pairs (Eq. 15, ratio of aligned-model to reference-model conditional probability) and, novelly, for the ENTIRE preference tuple (x, y_w, y_l) via the difference of the chosen and rejected probability ratios (Eq. 17). The tuple-level attack vector is presented as new relative to SFT/pretraining MIA frameworks that only consider prompt-response pairs.
threat_model: Attacker wants to determine whether a given preference record was part of the alignment (DPO/PPO) training set, at two granularities: (a) a single prompt-response pair, or (b) the full preference tuple (prompt, chosen response, rejected response). PREMIA assumes an 'optimistic' but realistic capability: white-box-style access to the aligned target model's output probabilities plus access to a reference model (the base pre-trained model or its SFT version) from which the target was fine-tuned. The authors justify this reference-access assumption via the common practice of aligning open-source base models locally (e.g., for regulatory/on-premise reasons), and frame PREMIA as a PRACTICAL UPPER BOUND on preference-data leakage rather than a black-box attack. It does not apply to closed-source models where the base model is inaccessible.
limitations: The authors explicitly do NOT evaluate any mitigations (they suggest DP-SGD, model pruning, and privacy-aware losses as future work). PREMIA depends on access to a base/SFT reference model, so it does not extend to closed-source LLMs, and being 'optimistic' it characterizes an upper bound rather than a realistic black-box adversary. Absolute AUROC gains for many baselines are modest (often 0.51-0.60), so the strong separation is driven largely by PREMIA itself. The theoretical results rest on simplifying assumptions (beta=0 regularization ignored, MALT constancy assumption, an i.i.d. likelihood assumption, and a stopping-criterion overfitting assumption in Theorem 2.1 that the authors note was added in a later revision). Experiments cover only two datasets (SE, IMDB) and single-epoch-style alignment.

datasets_used: Stack-Exchange-Paired (SE) - Stack Overflow Q&A pairs, more-voted answer preferred; data/rl split for training, data/evaluation for validation
IMDB-RLHF-Pair - positive-sentiment response preferred; 20k entries used for training, remainder for validation
quantitative_results: Mistral-7B on SE (Table 2, PREMIA): DPO vs PPO AUROC = MIAChosen 0.80 vs 0.52, MIARejected 0.76 vs 0.52, MIAPair 0.93 vs 0.52
GPT2-xl on SE (Table 4, PREMIA-SFT): MIAPair AUROC DPO 0.928 vs PPO 0.519; MIAChosen 0.858 vs 0.516
Mistral-7B-v0.1 on SE (Table 1, PREMIA-SFT): MIAChosen AUROC DPO 0.803 vs PPO 0.521
IMDB easy task (Table 4, PREMIA-SFT): Mistral-7B MIAPair AUROC DPO only 0.556 vs PPO 0.513; Open-llama-7b tuple 0.500
Reference-model swap robustness (Table 3): Open-llama-7b scored with Open-llama-3b as reference still gives SE tuple AUROC 0.882
Utility parity (Table 2): reward -1.035 (DPO) vs -0.771 (PPO) on Mistral-7B SE, i.e. PPO matches/exceeds DPO utility while leaking far less
Fig. 3: DPO-aligned Mistral-7B exceeds 90% train/eval discrimination accuracy within the first 0.2 epoch on IMDB
